name: AU Jason
body: 以現在網約車司機嘅數量或供應量來計算，每逢繁忙時間或在惡劣天氣情況下，很多時間已經不足夠應付市民嘅需求，要等頗長時間，或者已經找不到司機。強烈建議政府在各網約車平台蒐集這方面嘅真實嘅數據。 若果將來只有更少網約車司機，這一定會帶來對市民非常之不便，尤其對行動不便嘅人士，例如孕婦、老人家、傷殘人士等等。 中國大陸很多城市都已經有成功及完善嘅網約車法例和監管方法，強烈建議香港政府先參考這些城市嘅做法，然後再去立法監管香港網約車司機嘅數量。

name: 黃亨利
body: 根本不應限制網約車牌照上限，望約車的出現，原意就是增加市場競爭，令服務更加進步。設牌照上限，根本就是本末倒置，重蹈的士牌照的覆轍。

name: PANG Alex
body: 規管的應該是道路試用安全。 而不是數量。 本身網約車已經有比的士更完善的制度，既不能選擇目的地，又不能濫收費，表現差的自然被排斥(無論司機還是乘客也是)。這些完全將的士行業行比下去。 綜觀香港和日本的士業對比，未盲的也知道高下，連盲得也分得出車輛保養質素和司機駕駛技術。香港的士唔識路，兜路，唔過海，揀客拒載，近又唔去遠又唔去最好翻屋企先去，又一路揸一路食煙，手車又屎。 香港成就於自由經濟，supply應該由demand來決定。 如果網約車沒有競爭力，人們自然選擇坐錢七的士。 可以斷定，問題根本不是出在網約車，而是香港的士業實在太不堪。 司機操守和技術、車輛保養維護、行業內部剝削，真正需要糾正和整頓的應該是的士業。 而不是反過來壓制惠及人民的服務和產品。更應該進一步幫助網約車正規運作，例如保險業界應該推出網約車專用的行車保險。 煩請高管和每個月入十多萬的議員醒一醒，繼續維護既有利益者，也不是長治久安的做法。

name: 何潤雄
body: 強烈支持綱約車，1萬部實在不足夠，應該不限數量，積極不干擾政策，等待市場自然調節數量。的士唔會考慮乘搭，大部分司機冇禮貌，態度不佳，特別短途情況. 與規管網約車服務有關的4項附屬法例小組委員會KUNG Margaret於2026年6月9日提交的意見書Uber privides an excellent alternative to taxi. Taxi drivers in Hong zkong has theworst service of transportation. They have attutude problems, and too many of thedrivers are oo old to be in the trade.  This has been an ongoing issue for the past 40yearsOnly recently there has been a slight improvement after Uber was intruduced to themarket. The current planned allotment of 10000 for Uber  drivers is insuffient forthe market. I urge that the number increase to at least 25000. I do not want torevert back to the days where one has to put up with the rudeness, dishonesty andprice gouging of these taxi drivers. The market competition to survive andimprove. Major cities in  Mainland provide taxis and various car hailing service.Let the consumers have more choices. This the survival of the fittest theory. Thetaxi drivers should clean up their act and stop whining. Why more consumersprefer Uber is for the netter service quality they offer. The root of the problem iswith the Taxis. No point in asking the government to step in when they are notready to make any improvementsLC Paper No. CB(3)662/2026(105)

name: 郭芝芝
body: 本人近年對 市區的士體驗 多次感到失望， 除了 司機態度惡劣， 凌晨時分 和 某些地點 出現 全線冚旗， 揀乘客， 及私下加價， 就連 平台約車也內付30蚊小費， 要 加小費才接單， 的士已經每年加價 ，但越來越差， 反而uber 所有都完善 ，沒有 體驗過 爭吵成分， 懇請政府 重視 及增加Uber 網約車加入的權利 ，方便市民，謝謝

name: CONCERNED Citizen
body: Dear Chairman and Members of the Subcommittee, I am writing as a regular commuter and frequent user of ride-hailing services inHong Kong to express my strong opposition to the proposed subsidiaryregulations. Restricting ride-hailing vehicle permits to a strict quota of 10,000 via alottery system will not only harm consumers through higher prices and longer waittimes, but it will also force citizens back into relying on a traditional taxiecosystem that suffers from severe safety, quality, and accountability issues. While I support the government's intention to regulate the industry, the currentproposal protects an outdated monopoly at the expense of passenger safety andHong Kong’s international reputation. 1. Severe Safety and Accountability Deficits in Traditional Taxis The fundamental advantage of ride-hailing platforms like Uber is the digitalfootprint—every trip, driver, and vehicle is tracked via GPS in real-time.Traditional taxis completely lack this level of accountability, leading to serioussafety risks for passengers: Criminal Behavior and Lack of Redress: Because traditional taxi trips are oftenanonymous, passengers have little recourse when facing rogue drivers. I know ofegregious firsthand accounts where local cab drivers, alongside accomplices, haveattempted to physically assault and rob passengers. In a ride-hailing vehicle, thedriver’s identity, location, and route are permanently recorded and shareable withfamily or police, acting as a powerful deterrent against crime. No Reliable Way to Recover Lost Property: Traditional taxis lack a centralized,digital system for lost items. When passengers accidentally leave their belongingsbehind in a local cab, they are almost never recovered. Ride-hailing apps allow passengers to contact the driver immediately and trace the exact vehicle,safeguarding consumer property. 2. Dangerous Driving Habits Endangering Vulnerable Commuters The driving behavior of many traditional taxi drivers poses a direct physical hazardto Hong Kong residents, particularly vulnerable groups like the elderly. Erratic lane-changing, sudden braking, and aggressive, sporadic driving arerampant in the traditional taxi industry. I have seen how this dangerous driving directly jeopardizes the safety and physicalwell-being of elderly passengers, such as my friends' grandparents, who require asmooth and stable commute. Ride-hailing platforms utilize star-rating systems andtelematics to actively monitor and weed out dangerous drivers, ensuring asignificantly safer ride. 3. Substandard Vehicle Quality Damaging Hong Kong’s Image Hong Kong prides itself on being a world-class financial hub and a "Smart City."However, the general vehicle quality of the local taxi fleet is noticeably poor, withaging, poorly maintained, and unclean cars dominating the roads. Forcing tourists and residents to rely on a subpar taxi fleet actively degrades theimage of Hong Kong. Ride-hailing services consistently offer higher-quality, cleaner, and more modernvehicles, reflecting the standards that a global city should maintain. 4. Economic and Operational Flaws of the Current Proposal In addition to safety concerns, the mechanical aspects of the proposal are highlyflawed:  The 10,000-Permit Cap: A hard quota ignores market reality, ensuring thatpassenger demand will vastly outstrip supply during peak hours, bad weather, andholidays, leading to skyrocketing surge prices. The Lottery-Based System: Allocating permits by luck rather than merit or safetyrecords completely disincentivizes service quality. Conclusion and Recommendations Hong Kong citizens deserve the right to choose transport options that prioritizetheir personal safety, physical comfort, and digital accountability. By capping ride-hailing permits, the government is effectively trapping commuters in a market withfewer safe choices. I urge the Subcommittee to: Ditch the rigid 10,000 quota and lottery system in favor of an open, demand-ledlicensing framework. Prioritize passenger safety by legalizing a robust ride-hailing market that holdsdrivers accountable through modern technology. Thank you for your time and consideration of the views of Hong Kong commuters. Concerned Citizen

name: 鄭國鏢
body: 政府首階段發上限一萬個網約車牌照, 我認為不合理, 且有違網約車善用閒置資源的概念. 網約車的好處勢是利用閒置資源, 司機用不著私家車或沒其他用途時, 可駕駛網約車, 不一定會每天定時開工. 我認為不應為網約車牌照數量設置上限. 任何申請人只要合乎條件就可發牌. 現時網約車沒有數量上限, 但整體交通及日常出行大致正常.

name: 張德銓
body: I would support granting a much higher quota to rideservice providers such asUber. The fact is that the traditional Hong Kong taxi industry has failed the generalpublic for many years through consistently poor service standards, includingunprofessional driver manners, poorly maintained vehicles, frequent refusals ofservice, and a lack of accountability. Even today, if you randomly take a taxi, thereis still a high chance you will get into one of these poorly maintained vehiclesrather than a clean, comfortable, and safe car. In addition, cashless transactions arenow a basic expectation in a modern international city, and if not for thecompetitive pressure from Uber and similar platforms, the taxi industry wouldnever have moved toward offering such options to its customers. I sincerely urgethe Legislative Council to consider the longterm benefit for the general public overdated laws and regulations that have, for too long, primarily protected the taxiindustry instead of passengers. Thank You.

name: 鍾鳴
body: 我認為網約車牌照，最起碼要發行三萬個，否則只會減低約車的經驗，和令到價格搶高，對出行的體驗很差。

name: YEP Srisuda
body: There should be no quota for ride hailing. It will cost longer wait time and increasein price.

name: 蘇鍵鋒
body: 本人認為應提升網約車司機的名額， 讓網約車服務維持合理價格水平。有些圍村、鄉郊地方想call的士也沒有車會接單，回國內遊行也是用網約車，香港應該與時並進！

name: 林偉明
body: 本人認為網約車發牌名額應該與的士牌數目一樣，這才是公平競爭！事實係市民對網約車需求已比的士大！

name: MAK Sarah
body: I disagree with the quota of 10,000 as that limits the number of available apphailing rides available, and will increase my wait times and cause rates to increase.The government should leave the number to the actual demand and supply of thesociety and not interfere.

name: BUTT Bally
body: Ride Hailing should be control on a business level, not driver level.  Most new innovative ideas rest on sharing and leisure.  It is hard if not unfriendlyto control on a driver level.  On a business level, depending on their efficiency, willingness to resolve HongKong needs, can be assigned with certain level of active driver at specific time andday.  For example, during rush hours or holiday the Ride Hailing can be allowed withover 50,000 active driver.  The more a business is willing to fulfill this withoriginal or close to original price can be assigned with more quotas

name: 陳家杰
body: 我支持香港立法規管網約車，並認為在合規前提下，網約車在多方面可比的士更好，尤其在效率、安全與用戶體驗上能帶來提升。 首先，網約車的服務流程更透明、可追蹤。乘客通常可在出發前清楚掌握司機與車輛資訊、車牌號碼、接駁與行程時間等，行程亦有平台記錄。相比之下，的士雖然同樣有車牌與司機身份，但在叫車方式與資訊透明度上，往往較依賴現場與個別經驗。當制度要求平台提供可追溯紀錄並強化事故責任時，網約車的安全管理會更容易落實與監督。 其次，網約車在接駁效率與減少空跑方面更具優勢。平台可根據地點與需求提供叫車匹配，減少在路邊等待、也降低無目的行駛。這不但提升乘客體驗，也能在一定程度上減少路面浪費，讓整體交通運作更有效率。 第三，網約車的使用更符合多元需求。香港不同時段、不同地點對運輸的需求差異大，例如深夜、偏遠地區或繁忙時段。網約車透過電子化叫車與即時匹配，通常能更快提供服務，讓乘客在可預期的時間內出行。若立法要求清晰定價或透明收費機制，乘客就能更容易比較與理解車費，減少爭議。 第四，競爭可以推動整體服務質素提升。當網約車納入制度管理，符合條件的營運者會被要求遵守安全、保險、培訓與服務標準。這種「有規範的競爭」可促使的士服務在車況、守時、處理投訴等方面持續改善，而不是把資源浪費在灰色地帶的拉扯。 當然，前提是立法必須做得好：包括司機與車輛准入、保險責任、紀錄保存、投訴處理、打擊無牌與危險行為，以及合理的上落客與營運規範。只要規管到位，網約車就能在安全透明、叫車效率與整體體驗上勝過目前多依賴現場叫車的士，成為香港更值得推廣的交通選擇。

name: 何卉
body: I am writing to object to the proposed subsidiary legislation that limits the numberof ride-hailing vehicle permits to 10,000. As an ordinary citizen who relies on ride-hailing services for daily commuting, I believe this cap is detached from publicneeds and will make traveling in Hong Kong significantly harder.Please considerthe following daily realities faced by passengers:It is already too hard to catch aride: During morning and evening rush hours, finding a taxi or a ride-hailing car isalready a daily struggle. We are routinely met with "no cars available" screens orendless waiting times.Exorbitant peak-hour fares: Because demand vastly outstripssupply during peak periods, the surge pricing is already incredibly high. Cuttingthe current number of active drivers down to a strict 10,000 limit will only makecars scarcer and push surge fares to an absolute extreme.Part-time drivers keep thecity moving: The government expects 10,000 cars to handle all demand, but mostride-hailing drivers are part-time workers who only log on when they are free orduring peak hours. A cap on the number of permits means we lose the veryflexibility that makes ride-hailing work in the first place.Lack of transportalternatives: Traditional taxis alone cannot meet the massive public demand duringpeak periods or in specific districts. We need more cars on the road during rushhours, not fewer.Public transport policy should prioritize the convenience andpockets of the general public. Imposing a 10,000 cap will hurt ordinary commutersthe most. I strongly request the government to remove or substantially increase thislimit to protect our right to commute efficiently.

name: LUK Larry
body: 希望可增加網約車牌照至最少20000個，現時已經經常叫車很慢及截不到的士，如網幾車牌照減至10000個肯定會影響日常出行

name: 李雪雲
body: 我支持香港立法規管網約車，因為立法的目的不是簡單地放行或限制，而是把這類新型出行服務納入清晰、可執行的制度之中，讓乘客得到更安全、更可靠、也更透明的體驗。 首先，網約車涉及載客運輸，安全是最核心的前提。透過立法，可以要求司機與車輛必須符合基本準入條件，例如身份核實、必要培訓、車輛檢查以及保險安排；同時，制度亦能強化責任追溯機制。當發生事故或爭議時，若能有完整行程紀錄與可查核的資料，處理就會更有效率，乘客的安全感也會更高。相比只依靠臨場接觸與個別經驗的安排，合規後的網約車能把風險控制在更可管的範圍內。 其次，網約車的服務模式能提升出行效率，減少乘客不必要的等待。香港不同時段的交通需求差異很大，繁忙時段或特殊天氣下，叫車體驗往往不穩定。平台化的即時配對通常能更快找到合適的車輛，令乘客更容易預先安排時間；同時，若規管配套到位，也能在一定程度上減少無目的空跑，讓路面資源更有效運用。對市民而言，這不只是「方便」而是直接影響到通勤效率與生活節奏。 再者，立法能推動收費與服務透明化，減少不必要的爭拗。當規則清楚規定收費計算方法、必要費用處理與資訊披露，乘客就能更容易理解車資並作出選擇；而有紀錄可查的制度安排，也能讓投訴與查核更有依據。這種透明度，能夠降低臨場臆測與衝突，讓互動更有秩序。 此外，網約車對多元出行需求更具彈性。香港雖然公共交通網絡完善，但在深夜時段、較偏遠地點，或需要即時到達目的地、攜帶行李等情況，傳統交通模式未必總能提供同樣便利。網約車在合規前提下能補足這些「最後一段」的需求，使出行選擇更貼近市民日常生活的真實需要。 最後，立法規管有助建立更公平、可持續的競爭環境。當網約車被納入制度管理，守規者能在清晰標準下營運，而不合規者則能被更有效地打擊，從而 避免灰色地帶造成的混亂。長遠而言，制度化的競爭會促使服務質素提升，例如車況與守時、客戶服務水平、以及對投訴的回應效率，最終受益的仍是廣大乘客。 總結來說，我支持香港立法規管網約車，主張的核心是安全為先、透明為原則、可執行為底線。只要制度設計到位，包括準入與保險、紀錄保存、執法與收費透明等配套，網約車就能成為香港更可靠、更有效率的交通補充，讓市民出行更安心也更方便。

name: 吳錦龍
body:
不贊同發牌上限，因為 這跟共享經濟嘅原意善用閒置資源嘅初衷完全違背。 好似我呢啲平時。 每日可能開兩三個鍾，幫補家計亦都善用。 志願嘅人來講到漢。 這樣子立法同限制發牌數量。 不，但不能幫助改善任何情況。反而增加經濟壓力，立法嘅原意應該係令到社會更好更和諧，而唔係本末倒置。 LC Paper No. CB(3)662/2026(122)

To: Members of the Subcommittee on Subsidiary Legislation Relating to Ride-Hailing Services, I am writing to you as a Hong Kong resident to share my views on the recently proposed subsidiary regulations for ride-hailing services. While I support establishing a clear regulatory framework for the industry, I strongly oppose the proposed strict quota of 10,000 vehicle permits. As a citizen who regularly relies on ride-hailing platforms like Uber, I am deeply concerned that implementing such a severe cap on supply will negatively impact the daily lives of Hong Kongers in several ways:  Higher Costs for Citizens: A strict quota will artificially restrict the supply of available vehicles. Basic economics dictates that when demand remains high but supply is capped, prices will surge. Everyday citizens should not have to bear the burden of inflated transportation costs just to get around their own city.  Longer Wait Times: One of the primary reasons ride-hailing is so highly valued in Hong Kong is its efficiency and reliability. Limiting the number of vehicles to 10,000 will inevitably lead to driver shortages, especially during peak hours or poor weather, resulting in unacceptably long wait times.  Loss of Premium Service and Comfort: Many residents, including myself, actively choose Uber because it consistently provides a comfortable, high-quality, and premium riding experience. Constraining the market limits our choices as consumers and removes the competitive incentive that keeps service standards high. Ride-hailing has modernized how we travel, offering a level of comfort and convenience that the public greatly appreciates. Implementing a hard cap and a lottery-based allocation system feels like a step backward for a world-class city like Hong Kong. I urge the Legislative Council to reconsider the 10,000 vehicle quota. Please adopt a more flexible, market-driven approach that prioritizes consumer choice, keeps ride costs affordable, and ensures wait times remain short. Thank you for your time and for considering the voice of the commuting public. Sincerely, Johnson Chow

name: 偉邦劉
body: Currently we have over 30,000 ride-hailing drivers operating in HK.  I have seenno logical reason to reduce that number to a third (10,000 quotas).  I believe thegovernment should not balance the interests of different stakeholders (because thatcould be sacrificing the public interests and benefiting certain stakeholders e.g.Taxi owners) but focus on the actual needs of Hong Kong and citizens.  There areno benefits to the citizens in reducing the number of available drivers to 1/3 of thecurrent strength.   Also depends on the vetting of the license application, if we allocate the license inrandom, how do we prevent the taxi unions to hog all the licenses?  Is that a really‘fair’ system, or is it better to allocate all 30,000+ licenses to all current drivers andthen the government can control/manage the future growth through licensing. Why break the status quo and create transportation problems?

name: TANG Tze-hong
body: The arbitrarily set limit of 10,000 ride hailing licenses is an embarrassingdisappointment for HK for various reasons: *it undermines any credibility that HK claims to be forward thinking andinnovative (compare and contrast Singapore's highly supportive environment insetting up ride hailing with the appropriate infrastructure (eg Changi airportinfrastructure v HKIA's) compared to HK's stop start grudging approach) *it panders to vested interests that are themselves not keen to evolve and clearlywish to protect services that fail to satisfy the travelling public (ie current taxilicensing is an aging system that serves to protect certain commercial interestsrather than promote the service that is meant to be provided) *it seemingly does not provide for a mechanism for revision (at least this has notbeen mentioned) *a more meaningful way to address numbers may have been to set certainrequirements/standards that need to be met by licensees so that ride hailing can begrown in a controlled organic fashion rather than an arbitrary limit on licensesgranted  *an arbitrary limit suggests that the authorities haven't done their homework on thescale of ride hailing (despite the inordinate amount of time that has already passedsince ride hailing was available) and its success to date and the reasons for itspopularity compared to traditional services *if control of numbers is an aim there are - see above - more meaningful ways ofdoing so which also help to address standards and suitability of those seeking toprovide such services

name: 梁耀輝
body: 促請政府審慎考慮網約車牌照政策，保障的士業界合理權益政府十多年來對非法「白牌車」監管嚴重不力，管理制度充滿漏洞，警方打擊行動亦流於表面，未能根治問題。 這種長期的不作為，直接導致廣大的士車主與投資者蒙受巨大的經濟損失。不容忽視的是，這些投資者當初全都是拿出真金白銀、依法合規地投資的士牌照，如今卻要因政府的監管失職而承擔資產貶值的惡果，這對業界極其不公。 現時，政府在未有完善配套的情況下，計劃強行推出一萬個網約車牌照，此舉極度輕率。在目前經濟環境欠佳、整體消費力減弱的市道下，如果市場突然在短時間內湧入一萬輛合規網約車，將會對整個大眾運輸生態造成毀滅性的衝擊。 因此，政府必須慎重考慮以下核心訴求： 嚴格限制在線時間：政府必須對網約車設立嚴格的營運時間限制（控制在線時間）。 保護非繁忙時間生計：在非繁忙時間，市場客量本已供過於求。若不作分流與規管，前線的士司機的收入將會遭到毀滅性打擊，徹底失去維持基本生計的空間。 推行新政必須顧及現存業界的承受能力。本會強烈呼籲有關當局必須三思而後行，切勿輕率推動政策。否則，此舉不僅無法解決交通訴求，反而會徹底激化社會矛盾，打破現有的行業平衡，最終造成不可挽回、極度混亂的社會局面。

name: 吳韻超
body: 強烈建議uber的牌照不應設限，或至少應全部自動給予目前已經登記的uber司機。否則將做成服務大倒退，香港乘客得不到本身已經在享受的服務。

name: 彭師曉
body: 1.網約車基本上每一個國家都有，很多時候我們去旅遊也會經常用到的，點對點就到了的確很方便。在香港是一個旅遊之都如果沒有是真的會差一點。有時候語言不通用網約的是非常好，也不感覺自己被人騙了。透明度高。 2.出一萬個牌其實也不多，現在有很多的士司機也是在積極的做網約車的，其實我覺得是有些人不够積極的態度去工作，帶了很多情緒工作，香港是一福地啊！怎麼可能會沒工作，沒飯食？我覺得現在的人做得太少，想得太多又不懶得做，個個都想依賴政府，其實努力一點，萬事都有可能的達到的。現在有很多地方都請人啊，但是有很多都是想出現金的，不供強積金的，感覺現在的人都不知道怎麼想的！過份的好就變成養懶人，這些都是個人意見。

name: 鄭豪賢
body: 一個國家同一個城市有冇進步，就係睇吓佢有冇決心去改變，網約車正正就係反映一個城市有冇進步，當知道網約車發牌一萬個及條件非常奇怪，完全違反顛覆網約車嘅優勝之處，同的士有咩分別？ 例如頭尾玻璃要貼網約車適應嘅標誌，就係呢樣嘢已經反映得很奇怪，時代嘅轉變係可以睇好多書去知道，好多財經雜誌正正反映點解香港冇人願意搭的士，因為的士錯漏百出其實都唔使介紹，一個經濟學家講唔想搭的士嘅人越嚟越多嘅因素就係唔想俾人知自己搭緊的士個趨勢係去到醜，所以轉搭網約車而你們標籤網約車即係本末倒置，希望特區政府審慎考慮，專注喺用家這方面考慮。謝謝

name: LEBRUN Paul
body: The Government needs to understand that we, the commoners, are 100% fed upwith traditional taxis.  They have been the monopoly for point-to-pointtransportation service for a very long time; they have been left unchallenged andpoorly managed.  My personal experience with traditional taxis is equally poor.Old and poorly maintained cars, the rancid smell, the 5-6 mobile phone blockingthe windscreen, the texting and driving, hire refusals, cash only (yes some driversstill refuse e-paymetn although it is the Law), unsafe drivers (speeding, fallingasleep, too old to drive, don't care about other road users, poor driving technique,poor driving attitude!!!), and did I mention HIRE REFUSAL?  You can't blamethem; they have no incentive to improve, none whatsoever.  They were leftunchallenged.  You, as our government, must change this.  More competitionmeans better and cheaper service - simple as that.  Rise hail services are great, noguesswork and stand in the heat waiting for a taxi, plus so many other benefits.You might not have taken a taxi in a while because you probably have drivers, Iencourage you to take traditional taxis exclusively for 1 month, then you'll know.

name: DREVER Mathew
body: As a Hong Kong resident I enjoy one of the most affordable and convenient taxisystems in the world in a city where it is difficult to own a car.  Please don't put alimit on the number of taxi's as it would make them harder to find and increase theprices.  My wife and I use the taxi system if we're working a lot of overtime or workbeyond our last bus, and it's really important for the taxi ride to be affordable.Please don't change that.

name: 麥汝成
body: 我個人覺得以什麼角度去看待網約車合法化的正確方案,應該以用家和賴以為生的工作者出發,並不是單就車主的利益為大前提。兩者都是服務香港每日上班上學和外來的遊客出行提供服務！如果的士和網約車可以並行提供服務！而的士與網約車兩幫司機其實可以互相替保,亦即是的士有較好的經營條件時,網約車司機可以去揸的士,相返的士司機亦可参加網約行列!能做到這個靈活性大概都可以解決謀生司機營業需求。再想,假使網約車一萬個名額只得3000個在街上行走其餘七千個只係兼職而不提供服務試問一個國際城市外國人用慣的系統一下子用不上這不是一個荒天下之大謬嗎?我知道政府對每一個行業都有責任去保護因為香港嘅經濟同市民嘅就業係息息相關,但係買入的士牌照投資,呢啲係個人意願.舉例我97年買咗一間住所但在03年下跌,我把住所都出售並虧損很多,這個政府也願意為我埋單嗎?認清楚工作崗位需要保護,投資帶來的損失,不應該受到保護！政府應該活化這個行業並不是向其中一方打壓這才是黃道！

name: 楊肇民
body: 本人認為，政府現行規管方案過於保守，與國家及全球積極發展數字經濟、共享經濟的潮流背道而馳：落後於時代潮流：內地及全球網約車早已全面普及，成為市民出行常態。香港若實施嚴格總量管控及「人車綁定」，無異於削足適履，令智慧城市發展倒退。扼殺兼職與閒置運力：共享經濟的核心在於善用社會閒置資源。政府強推全職化規管，剝奪了市民工餘兼職賺取收入的彈性，也無法有效應對惡劣天氣或節假日的出行高峰。強烈敦促政府順應科技與時代進步，放寬限制，還市民現代化的出行選擇。

name: MOHAMMAD Muzamil
body: I do **not support** the proposal’s strict quota of **10,000 vehicle permits for allride-hailing services**, and I have serious concerns about the way it would beimplemented and governed. First, a hard cap of 10,000 permits is too blunt an instrument for a market thatneeds to respond to real, changing demand. Ride-hailing demand is not static: itvaries by time of day, day of week, major events, weather, public transitdisruptions, and seasonal shifts. When supply is capped permanently, the systemwill struggle to match those fluctuations. That mismatch typically shows up aslonger wait times, reduced availability in higher-demand areas, and higher pricesfor riders—regardless of whether the quota is justified as “balancing supply.”Instead of encouraging efficient service, the quota risks creating avoidablecongestion in the streets and at pickup points, as drivers and riders compete forlimited capacity. Second, limiting permits across **all ride-hailing services** can unintentionallydiscourage innovation and competition. Even if the intent is to control congestionor emissions, a strict cap can push companies to compete less on service qualityand more on compliance strategies and lobbying for favorable outcomes in theallocation process. That matters because ride-hailing has the potential to improvelast-mile connectivity and reduce some private car usage. If the policy environmentpunishes expansion through a fixed quota, the sector may stagnate—especially inneighborhoods that are underserved or where service declines as soon as capacitybecomes tight. Third, the proposal’s **lottery-based allocation mechanism** is not an effective orfair way to allocate a scarce public-interest resource. A lottery is random and doesnot reward entities that can demonstrate better safety records, labor protections,vehicle quality, training standards, or measurable compliance with emissionsrequirements. In practice, randomness can place well-equipped and responsible operators at the same starting line as operators that are less prepared to meet long-term obligations. This could lower the overall standard of service and compliance,because the selection process does not reflect performance or readiness. A lottery can also create perverse incentives. Companies and applicants may focuson gaming the system rather than investing in improvements that benefit thepublic. For example, applicants might structure their operations around winningthe lottery rather than building stable networks, reducing dead miles, or improvingaccessibility. Over time, the city could end up with a permit pool that is formal butnot operationally optimal—meaning fewer gains in safety, sustainability, or riderexperience than the policy claims to deliver. Fourth, the proposal relies on a **dynamic adjustment mechanism** to respond toconditions, but combining a strict cap with periodic adjustment still risksundermining the purpose of flexibility. If the city has the ability to adjust based ondemand and congestion indicators, then the fixed quota is unnecessary—or at leastshould be substantially higher with a responsive framework that can tighten supplyonly when evidence supports it. Otherwise, the city may still be constrained by theinitial “ceiling,” even when data shows that additional permits could reduce streetcongestion by preventing long waits and reducing roaming behavior where driverssearch inefficiently for riders. Moreover, dynamic adjustment mechanisms can be difficult to design in ways thatare transparent and predictable to the industry and riders. If adjustment rules areopaque or politically influenced, participants may face uncertainty and instability.Instability can reduce investment in driver recruitment, vehicle upgrades,accessibility features, and service coverage in lower-income areas. Thisundermines the public interest goal because it can harm exactly the communitiesthat rely most on reliable ride-hailing service when public transit is limited. Fifth, the quota may shift problems rather than solve them. When permits areconstrained, riders who cannot easily find an available vehicle may resort toalternatives such as unregulated rides, illegal pick-ups, or other forms of riskytransportation. That can weaken enforcement outcomes, reduce rider safety, and make it harder for the city to monitor service quality. Additionally, permitconstraints can lead to more “dead time” and “dead miles” as drivers reposition tofind demand—potentially increasing emissions rather than reducing them. In summary, I do not support the **10,000 permit cap** because it is overly rigid,risks harming competition and service coverage, uses a **lottery** that does notprioritize safety or performance, and may not achieve better congestion orenvironmental outcomes once real-world behavior is considered. If the city’s goalsare congestion reduction, safety improvement, and emissions control, thoseobjectives can be pursued with more targeted measures—such as performance-based licensing, stronger enforcement of vehicle standards, congestion pricingstrategies, real-time permit rebalancing, and incentives for lower-emissionfleets—without imposing a fixed ceiling that constrains the entire marketregardless of conditions.

name: 馮文
body: 此將直接限制未來的車輛供應，並影響我的乘車體驗。這可能意味著未來配對車輛的時間更長、價格更高，以及出行選擇減少。

name: 李志成
body: 希望網約車牌照數目沒有上限，因為全職兼職司機數目不只一萬個，且繁忙時間或地點偏遠，現有的士數量不足應付。 加上引入競爭可以提升服務質素，及為乘客帶來更有吸引力的收費。

name: 陳市民
body: 本人身為長期依賴點對點交通出行的香港市民，現就政府計劃規管網約車平台及牌照數量的政策，提交以下意見。 一、 傳統的士服務滯後，難以滿足市民期望多年來，香港傳統的士的服務質素一直為人詬病。「拒載」、「揀客」、「兜路」以及「態度惡劣」等行業亂象屢見不鮮。尤其在惡劣天氣、繁忙時間或偏遠地區，市民往往面臨「有車不願載」或被無理加價的困境。部分傳統的士車廂衞生欠佳，且拒絕接受電子支付，嚴重脫離香港作為國際金融及智慧城市的形象。 二、 網約車（Uber）具備透明、優質與現代化的絕對優勢相較之下，以Uber 為代表的網約車平台透過科技手段，徹底解決了上述痛點：定價透明： 乘客在行程開始前已知曉確切車資，免除被「濫收車資」的疑慮。電子支付： 支援多種無現金支付方式，迎合本地市民與海外旅客的消費習慣。雙向評價機制： 平台設有機制約束司機行為，車廂普遍乾淨，司機禮貌且樂意配合導航，出行體驗高下立判。三、 促請放寬發牌上限，保障市民出行選擇權政府早前考慮將網約車車輛許可證數量限制在約 10,000 至15,000 個，本人對此深表憂慮。若發牌數量顯著低於市民與旅客的實質出行需求，不僅會導致尖峰時段叫車成功率大幅下跌，更會推高車費，最終懲罰的。行政長官曾強調點對點交通政策「必須站在人民一邊」。唯有透過引進健康的市場競爭，給予網約車合法且充足的發展空間，才能倒逼傳統的士行業進行真正的服務改革。懇請政府傾聽主流民意，重審並調高網約車許可證上限，保障廣大市民靈活出行的基本權利。一名期望交通進步的香港市民

name: 張英才
body: 我不支持對網約車進行規管，「全港僅設 10,000 個配額上限、採抽籤分配、並以動態調整機制」的做法，我表示保留甚至擔憂。原因很簡單：這種設計很可能先行把供應限制在一個固定天花板，令未來需求增加時仍無法及時補足，最終反映到乘車時間更長、價格更高，並削弱市民的出行選擇。 網約車本質上是即時配對服務，需求會因時間、地點、天氣與大型活動而急劇波動。若總量被硬性鎖定在 10,000 上限，即使市場上有更多符合規定的司機與車輛，也未必能有效加入服務，供需之間就更容易出現短期缺口。當缺口出現，最直接受影響的是乘客：候車時間延長、配對成功率下降、在繁忙或偏遠地區更難叫到車。這些影響並不抽象，而是每天都會發生的體驗問題。 至於用抽籤分配配額，雖然在程序上被視為「公平」，但抽籤未必能公平地分配資源到真正需要的地區與時段。結果可能造成服務在某些時候與空間「供不應求」：乘客等得更久、成本更高，轉而尋找替代方案或被迫接受較差服務。當供應偏緊時，價格上升的壓力也會更大，尤其在公共交通較不便或需求突增時更明顯。 我亦關注「動態調整機制」能否有效對沖上述風險。若動態調整只是形式上的機制，缺乏清晰指標、調整頻率與透明度，就難以在需求明顯上升時迅速增加供應。更合理的做法，是以可量化的乘客體驗指標作為調整依據，例如平均候車時間、接駁成功率、取消率與尖峰缺口，並確保調整能快速、可預期地執行，而不是拖延或被上限因素過度限制。 因此，我希望當局在審視配額上限與分配方式時，能把「可達性」和「乘客體驗」置於首位，而不只是考慮管理成本。若要維持配額制度，至少應確保配額不會在早期就成為供應瓶頸；抽籤不應是唯一工具，亦應讓配額與服務表現或地區需求有更密切關聯；動態調整機制則要具體、透明，並能快速反應市場變化。否則，立法規管本應帶來更安全、更可靠的出行選擇，卻可能因供應受限而讓體驗變差。  我誠摯提出上述意見，請在制定附屬法例時更充分考慮乘客的實際需要，讓網約車在香港既能被妥善規管，也能真正做到「用得上、等得起、叫得到、收費更可接受」。

name: 黎玉
body: 毋須設定網約車輛許可證配額上限 與規管網約車服務有關的4項附屬法例小組委員會HASSAN Ali先生於2026年6月9日提交的意見書In my opinion, regulating ride-hailing services is a very good measure taken by theHKSAR Government. However, there few things i disagree or have an opinion about.Breakdown:1: Most of uber drivers work part-time rather than full-time so limiting the permitquota to 10,000 will be quite unfair as there are more than 30,000 uber drivers bothfull-time and part-time. The gap is huge. If there are no filters, part time driverscan be the first ones to get the permit whereas those who have only uber as theirsole income might have to find another job. Therefore, my suggestion is that thequota shall be increased by atleast double and priority should be given to full-timedrivers. While I agree that permit for everyone is also not a good solution;government can come up with a better strategy without making uber driversnervous that they may end up losing their job.2: The permit fees should be very well structured as an ordinary uber driver can’tpay in thousands for a permit thats lasts 5 years.The HKSAR can come up with abetter solution/plan regarding the permit fee.3: Uber like in other countries or regions should be fully functional at all times andnot just peak hours. Hong Kong has one of the best transportation network andthere are many people who take different mode of transport. As a tourist landmark,uber has high potential to run full time like any other taxi in Hong Kong. Gigeconomy in Hong Kong has increased year on year and many choose to work ingig economy due to unemployment. Thus, treating uber as a normal work type isimportant and not limiting the hours of operation.4:There is no hard feeling towards any taxi driver as all of them are trying to earn aLC Paper No. CB(3)662/2026(139) living; but most of them are above the age of 65 and at that age one is not sotrustworthy on the road. Most of the road accidents are linked with taxi driverswhile some are fatal whereas there are rare cases regarding uber drivers and mostof them are very professional when working  This is merely a suggestion regarding ride-hailing service and I respect the stepstaken by the HKSAR Government while legalising ride-hailing services.

name: AUSTIN Keith
body: This submission is formally lodged to express my strong objection to the proposedlegislation aiming to cap ride-hailing licences at 10,000 via a ballot system. Whileregulation is necessary to ensure safety and quality standards, the implementationof an arbitrary numerical cap and a lottery-based allocation system will severelydamage Hong Kong’s transport ecosystem. This policy will create acute transportshortages, diminish service quality, harm the local economy, and severelyundermine Hong Kong’s reputation as a world-class, tech-forward tourismhub.The proposed 10,000 licence cap fails to account for the actual dailycommuting demands of Hong Kong's seven million residents. Artificiallyrestricting the supply of ride-hailing vehicles will lead to chronic deficits duringpeak hours, inclement weather, and in underserved districts, such as parts of theNew Territories and Kowloon East. Residents will face prolonged wait times andexorbitant surge pricing. Furthermore, ride-hailing is not a luxury but a vital utilityfor families with young children, the elderly, and persons with disabilities who findtraditional public transport or standard taxis physically challenging to navigate.Capping these services directly reduces their mobility and quality of life.Allocating licences via a ballot system treats a vital public service like a lottery. Itcompletely ignores operator merit, vehicle quality, and driver experience, allowingluck rather than market demand or service capability to dictate who operates.AsHong Kong actively positions itself to attract high-spending international andmega-event tourists, transport accessibility remains a critical pillar of the visitorexperience. International travelers and visitors from the Mainland expect seamless,app-based mobility solutions. They are accustomed to the reliability of digital ride-hailing platforms that eliminate language barriers, offer transparent cashlesspricing, and provide GPS-tracked safety. Forcing tourists arriving at Hong KongInternational Airport, the West Kowloon High-Speed Rail Station, or cruiseterminals into lengthy, frustrating queues due to a shortage of ride-hailing vehiclessends a damaging message. It signals that Hong Kong is structurally unequipped tohandle modern travel preferences and damages the city's first impression.Restricting digital transport solutions directly contradicts the Government’s own Smart City Blueprint and paints Hong Kong as a regressive market that protectslegacy monopolies at the expense of technological innovation and consumerconvenience.A ballot system also introduces severe market distortions thatundermine local economic health. Much like the current taxi licence premiumsystem, an arbitrary cap invites speculative behavior where licence winners maychoose to rent out their credentials rather than operate them, driving up costs fordrivers and passengers alike. Additionally, the flexible, gig-economy nature ofride-hailing provides vital supplementary income for thousands of local drivers. Arigid ballot eliminates this flexibility and forces part-time drivers out of the marketentirely, hurting local livelihoods.Instead of a restrictive cap and ballot system, theLegislative Council should consider a progressive regulatory framework. Licencesshould be issued based on strict vehicle safety standards, driver backgroundchecks, and service ratings rather than a lottery. If a cap must exist, it should bedynamically adjusted using real-time transport data to match actual commuter andtourist demand. The government should encourage healthy competition byderegulating certain aspects of the traditional taxi industry alongside legalizingride-hailing, thereby raising the bar for point-to-point transport across the board.An arbitrary 10,000 licence cap allocated by ballot is a blunt instrument that willyield net-negative outcomes for Hong Kong. It will penalize local commuters,alienate international tourists, and stymie the city’s economic evolution. I urge theLegislative Council to reject this proposal and instead pursue a smart, open, anddemand-driven regulatory approach that safeguards consumer choice andmaintains Hong Kong's status as a premier global destination.

name: 黃展昊
body: 香港市民普遍對的士司機極度失望, 煩請委員會考慮為什麼香港市民寧願附更多的價錢乘搭網約車, 也不會去乘搭的士。 儘管現時有嚴格法例規定及監管, 但的士司機的質素仍然十分參差。 短途、長途、交更時間、過海不接, 態度問題, 兜路, 強制上捨問題等 即使我要會限制規管, 最根本為什麼香港市民不選擇的士的原因就是因為的士司機自身的態度 當然也有優質的的士司機, 但是, 一日的士司機沒有更有效的規範,  網約車仍然每次不少市民的首選 煩請委員會考慮此因數, 不會過份限制數目

name: 黃小姐
body: The reason of allowing Ride-Hailing is to benefit hk resident have more choice fortransportation, expecially in peak time, if Nos. of license very limited, the purposeof helping us become fail. Many thanks

name: CHAN Joyce
body: Limiting the vehicles available makes consumer less likely to use ride hailing atall, which would also affect taxi drivers since there is less demand.

name: 李婉容
body: 我不理解立法當局希望以配額與抽籤方式來管理網約車數量，若全港以附屬法例建議的方式，將網約車許可證設為「僅 10,000 個上限」並採抽籤分配，且配合「動態調整機制」，存在明顯風險：它可能在供應未充分應對需求變化時，先行把整個市場的車輛供應上限固定下來，結果不但影響乘車體驗，也可能推高價格並降低選擇。 我最擔心的是，配額上限會直接限制未來車輛供應的彈性。網約車需求本就會隨時間與地點波動，例如繁忙時段、節假日、天氣突變或大型活動後，需求往往瞬間上升。在這種情況下，如果車輛總量被固定在 10,000 的上限，即使有新的合資格營運者或司機加入，也可能因配額不足而進不來，導致可用車輛不足、配對時間延長，令乘客的等待成本增加。 其次，抽籤方式分配配額即使在形式上公平，也不等於能有效反映市場需求與服務品質。抽籤結果可能令部分年份或地區出現供需錯配：有些時間與區域車隊不足，乘客就需要等待；有些時段車輛過剩，又可能導致營運壓力轉嫁到消費端。當供應偏緊時，價格上升的風險會更高，尤其在夜間或交通接駁較弱的情況下，乘客對可替代方案的選擇亦更少。 我亦希望「動態調整機制」能在概念上落實得更清楚、更可預期。若機制只停留在文字描述，而缺乏透明的調整指標、時程與上調上限，便難以真正緩解配額過緊帶來的問題。動態調整應能以可量化的指標運作，例如平均候車時間、接單成功率、取消率、尖峰需求缺口等，並在需求出現壓力時能快速增加供應，而不是多年才調整一次。 因此，我認為在考慮配額時，應把乘客體驗與可達性放在核心位置，而非單靠一刀切的上限與抽籤。若一定要設定配額，至少應確保：配額上限不會過早成為供應瓶頸；抽籤能與服務表現或地區需求掛鈎；以及動態調整機制具透明度、可量化指標、快速反應能力。否則，網約車本應帶來的效率與便利，反而會因供應受限而被削弱。  我的立場是：請在制度設計上優先保障乘客能更快配對、更合理收費與更多出行選擇，同時確保規管能達到安全與責任可追溯的目的。請讓配額與分配機制真正做到「以需求與服務表現為導向」，而不是先限制供應、再寄望後續調整能補救。我的聲音希望能被納入考慮，讓香港的網約車服務未來更貼近市民需要。

name: TSANG Edward
body: 本人歡迎正式接受網約車作為載客的方法，與國際、國內接軌，避免令香港成為落後地區。 但是次所批的數量過少，令人失望！觀乎全國、以至全世界，透過網上預約，提供點對點載客才是最新大趨勢。有限的服務、非國際品牌平台，只會進一步削弱香港吸引外地旅客的能力。 政府宜當機立斷，一步到位，避免拖泥帶水，吃力不討好。

name: BIRD Daniel
body: Ride-hailing services in Hong Kong are absolutely essential. I have lived in HongKong for over twenty years and seen a gradual decrease in the quality of publictaxis. Drivers are often rude, they have a poor manner, and often drive recklessly.On many occasions they refuse fares or to travel directly to destinations they donot wish to. Since the introduction of ride-hailing apps in Hong Kong I've receivedbetter service, safer driving, and more convenience that should suit a modern citylike Hong Kong. Such services offer a much needed transparency, particularly fornon-locals. By giving taxi drivers the opportunity to participate in schemes withride-hailing apps they will be required to drive better. The app also overcomesissues with translation as many Hong Kong taxi drivers do not speak English. Amodern city like Hong Kong must remain competitive. Whenever I travel to othercountries I rely on such apps to ensure safe travel for myself and my family. Iknow who the driver is and that they're connected to a system that helps guaranteesafety. Reducing the number of drivers available is a poor decision that desperatelyneeds reconsideration from public consultation.

name: 余家輝
body: As a regular Uber passenger in Hong Kong, I’m deeply disappointed by thegovernment’s decision to limit ride-hailing drivers to just 10,000—a quota thatUber itself warns will severely hurt service reliability and affordability. Accordingto Uber’s official estimation, if the quota is capped at this level (or even at 15,000),four out of every 10 ride requests during peak hours could fail, passenger waitingtimes will double, and fares could surge by as much as 70%. With more than30,000 drivers currently earning flexible income on Uber’s platform, this quotawould immediately cut active drivers by over 50%, creating a massive supplyshock. Rather than imposing an arbitrary cap that harms passengers and driversalike, the quota should not exist at all—ride-hailing supply should be controlled bythe market, where demand naturally determines how many drivers join, ensuringreliable service, fair prices, and sustainable earning opportunities for everyone.

name: TSANG C
body: To whom it may concern, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regardingthe current state of taxi services and the importance of ride-hailing platforms likeUber, which I have been using for over a decade. Uber's arrival was nothing shortof a lifesaver for many of us. Frankly, it is impossible for the government to be unaware of the persistent issueswith taxi services—such as poor driver attitudes, selective picking of passengers,and outright refusal of service. These problems are particularly glaring on Fridayswhen hailing a taxi becomes an ordeal, as most drivers turn off their meters tocherry-pick passengers. On Saturday afternoons, when families like mine want togo out together, the situation is even worse. While six-seater taxis do exist, theyare exceedingly rare. Have you ever tried booking one? It's nearly impossiblewithout paying an additional $80 on top of the metered fare. Why should ordinary citizens be left at the mercy of such practices? A typicalprivate car can only accommodate four passengers, which means a family of fourplus a domestic helper has no choice but to rely on Uber. Otherwise, we are forcedto pay exorbitant surcharges just to get a taxi—an unreasonable and often futileeffort. Beyond that, I must ask: why restrict ride-hailing platforms to a mere 10,000licences? Is the intention to push even Uber into a situation where drivers startcherry-picking passengers and citizens are forced to pay inflated fares? That woulddefeat the very purpose of having an alternative in the first place. Moreover, Uber's point-to-point service has been incredibly helpful for workingprofessionals like myself who need to arrange transport for elderly parents.Sometimes my parents may not know the exact address, but with Uber, I can book the ride for them directly—whether it is for a medical follow-up or simply gettingthem where they need to go. The same goes for sending our domestic helper withthe children to tutoring classes; it is a simple and convenient solution that taxissimply cannot reliably provide. In fact, since Uber became available, I have hardly driven my own car at all. Thishas effectively reduced the number of vehicles on the road. Isn't that one of thebenefits the government should be encouraging? The question remains: does the government truly aim to improve transportation, ordoes it tacitly allow taxi cartels to exploit citizens in genuine need? I urge the relevant authorities to seriously address these issues and create a fairer,more reliable transportation system for all. Thank you for your attention, and I look forward to hearing your thoughts. Best regards, Ms Tsang

name: 張兆鈞
body: 網約車輛許可證配額不應設有上限，如果真的要設有上限，網約車配額數量應該要跟的士牌照配額相若，以提供公共競爭的環境，由市場決定孰優孰劣

name: TSOI Siu-yam
body: 1. Executive SummaryAs a daily commuter and a member of the general public, Iwelcome the Government’s move to formalize a regulatory framework for point-to-point ride-hailing services. However, the ultimate success of this legislationdepends entirely on how the Road Traffic (Limit on Number of Ride-hailingVehicle Permits) Notice (L.N. 63 of 2026) is implemented.I urge theSubcommittee to advocate for an open, market-driven quota system rather than arestrictive cap. Restricting ride-hailing permits shields the traditional taxi industryfrom healthy competition. It harms the public interest. Keeping ride-hailingaccessible will raise service quality, expand transport capacity, and build a world-class smart city.2. Enhancing Service Quality Through Market CompetitionForyears, Hong Kong passengers have voiced frustrations over point-to-pointtransport services. Common complaints include refused rides, cherry-pickingpassengers during peak hours, and poor driver attitudes.The entry of ride-hailingplatforms like Uber introduced long-overdue accountability to the market:Two-Way Rating Systems: Drivers and passengers rate each other. This creates a self-regulating incentive for courteous and safe behavior.Upfront Pricing and DigitalRouting: Pre-calculated fares and GPS routing eliminate overcharging andarbitrary detours.Cashless Transactions: Digital payments offer a seamless, secure,and modern alternative to traditional cash transactions.Shielding standard taxisfrom this competition removes their incentive to innovate or improve. Embracingride-hailing platforms forces the entire point-to-point transport sector to elevate itsstandards. The consumer wins.3. Boosting Transport Quantity and Meeting PeakDemandHong Kong’s public transport system is highly efficient, but it faces severecapacity strains during peak windows. During heavy rainstorms, Friday rush hours,or late-night periods after trains stop running, finding a standard taxi is notoriouslydifficult.An artificial cap on ride-hailing permits will replicate the exact supplybottlenecks that plague the traditional taxi system. Mainstreaming ride-hailingplatforms offers crucial structural advantages:Dynamic Supply Matching: Ride-hailing relies on flexible, part-time, and full-time drivers who enter the marketexactly when demand spikes.Geographic Coverage: Ride-hailing algorithms efficiently dispatch vehicles to underserved areas outside core commercialdistricts.Relieving Mass Transit Pressure: A robust ride-hailing fleet provides areliable backup network, easing pressure on the MTR and bus networks duringdisruptions.4. Protecting the Livelihoods of Flexible WorkersThe ride-hailingeconomy supports thousands of drivers who rely on platforms for flexible income.Many drivers are self-employed individuals, parents, or part-time workersbalancing multiple responsibilities.Imposing an overly restrictive permit cap willdestroy these flexible livelihood opportunities. It risks driving the serviceunderground or forcing drivers out of work entirely. A fair and inclusive permitsystem protects these workers, brings them into a legal framework, and ensuresthey contribute positively to Hong Kong's economy.5. Recommendations for theSubcommitteeTo ensure the subsidiary legislation serves the public interest, Irequest the Subcommittee to consider the following recommendations:Maximizethe Permit Quota (L.N. 63 of 2026): Set the initial limit on Ride-hailing VehiclePermits high enough to absorb the existing pool of active, highly-rated drivers. Donot artificially choke the market.Implement an Automated, Data-Driven QuotaReview: Do not rely on static caps. The Transport and Logistics Bureau should usereal-time platform data (such as wait times and unfulfilled ride requests) to adjustpermit numbers dynamically based on public demand.Streamline the PermitProcess: Ensure that the permit application fees and bureaucratic requirementsoutlined in L.N. 61 of 2026 are reasonable. They should not act as a barrier toentry for individual, everyday drivers.6. ConclusionHong Kong aims to be aleading international financial center and a champion of smart city technology. Arestrictive approach to ride-hailing contradicts these ambitions.By keeping ride-hailing licenses widely accessible, the Government will foster a competitiveenvironment that naturally improves public transport quality and quantity. I urgethe Subcommittee to prioritize passenger choice, commuter convenience, andmarket innovation when vetting these pieces of subsidiary legislation.

name: 袁天賦
body: 不要監管就係最好

name: 姜培志
body: 我覺得10,000個執照實在太少，咁樣會導致call車費用增加。政府不應只顧的士團體嘅利益，亦要考慮到市民對網約車嘅需求。其實由市場下如果的士能夠做得好，根本網約車就唔會存在！為何網約車會在市場上存在？好明顯地因為的士司機質素參差。 所以10,000個執照實在太少！未能應付到市民對網約車嘅需求！

name: 劉華
body: 網約車立法，請以廣大市民想法為福祉，不要因為的士團體撐政府，政府就鬆章給他們。我相信尊貴的立法會議員，如從的士和網約車中間二選一，絕大多數都選擇網約車的。你們的真實想法，相信就是市民的真實想法，請放寬網約車發牌至少20,000個。請為市民着想。

name: 李绵楷
body: 本人李绵楷，谨以个人身份就规管网约车服务的4项附属法例提交以下意见。 一、支持网约车合法化及适度规管 网约车平台在全球多个城市已证明能提供便捷、高效的出行选择，补充传统公共交通不足。香港应建立清晰的发牌制度，让合规网约车与的士共存、良性竞争，最终提升整体服务质素。 二、建议发牌框架要点 1. 平台持牌：要求网约车平台向运输署申请经营牌照，需满足车辆安全、司机背景审查、保险覆盖、数据私隐保护等基本条件。 2. 车辆许可：用于网约服务的私家车应转换为“预约车辆许可证”，并设定合理车龄、定期验车及第三者保险要求（保险可参照的士但调整保费）。 3. 司机资格：需持有有效驾驶执照并通过网上平台专项培训（包括服务态度、路线选择、紧急应变），不强制要求持有的士牌照，但需无严重交通违例及犯罪记录。 4. 运力及收费规管：为避免过度挤占道路资源，初期可设定平台车辆总数上限；车费应由平台动态定价，但需清晰展示收费明细，并禁止低于成本的恶性补贴。 三、处理与的士业关系 建议设立过渡期（例如18个月），让现役网约车及司机逐步符合新规。同时，应同步推进的士业改革，如放宽的士颜色限制、引入电子召车及评分机制，促进公平竞争。  四、执法与申诉机制 对非法白牌车（未持任何许可）应加强执法；但已合规登记的网约车不应视为违法。设立独立申诉机制，处理市民或司机对规管执行的争议。 五、数据及安全要求 平台须将行程数据加密存储并定期提交运输署作统计用途，同时保障用户私隐。强制安装行车记录仪（非云端实时监控，仅在事故时提取），保障司机与乘客权益。 结语 规管网约车的核心目标是“安全、有序、便民”，而非简单取缔。期望小组委员会参考内地（如深圳、上海）及新加坡经验，制定既切合香港实际、又具前瞻性的附属法例。

name: CLARKE Martin
body: The market has spoken and Uber has won. I live in the NT and the taxis are old, (not an airbag in sight in most of them) thedrivers very old and  they are  operating under a system that promotes fast drivingand as many mobile phones as you can fit on a dashboard.. Over the years the government has been happy to allow the taxi service to decay soas not to annoy licence-holders (rentier income wins again). And now we aresupposed to be thrilled that taxis accept payment in something other than cash. Elsewhere in the world Uber and similar compete on price. In Hong Kong they areoften more expensive but have a better service.This tells you all you need to know..

name: 溫梓杰
body: The quota of 10,000 is a terrible idea that guarantees deteriorated passengerexperience and a sickening experience for everyone in HK who use a ride sharingservice.  It damages the image of HK as a global metropolitan and put a danger tothe sense of belonging of the locals. I’m urging you to raise the quota to 20,000.

name: 劉志華
body: I have been using Uber service for my daily commute. I found their cars are mostlyclean and driver courteous. I wish I can say the same thing for the taxi. Taxidrivers are mostly rude, impatience and drive aggressively. I feel strongly that thetaxi drivers drove themselves out of competition. Please do not limit the number ofUber license to 10,000. This will hurt the image of Hong Kong as visitors from allover the world are used to the Uber services. Poor taxi services in Hong Kong haveso much to catch up.

name: NGAI Ming-kit
body: Please approve adequate licenses for Uber to operate in Hong Kong.  As aninternational city, convenience and flexibility of transportation is very important tosustain the world most competitive and growing financial city.

name: 黎梓勤
body: 主席及各位委員公鑒： 本人為香港市民，亦是網約車平台（滴滴出行及高德地圖）的每日頻繁用戶。就政府現正審議規管網約車服務的四項附屬法例，本人特此提交以下意見，期望政府能落實「以民為本」的政策，建立安全、便利且具競爭力的點對點交通市場。 一、 促請取消發牌上限，實行市場化供求機制 現時市民對個人化交通的需求極為龐大，特別是在惡劣天氣、上下班繁忙時間及傳統交通工具難以覆蓋的地區。若政府強行設立僵化的牌照配額上限，將帶來以下弊端： 1 供求失衡： 限制數量將直接導致繁忙時間候車時間延長，無法滿足市民出行訴求。 2 助長炒賣： 固定的配額容易淪為炒賣工具，使牌照背離服務市民的初衷。 因此，本人強烈建議政府將發牌上限提升至「無限」。只要司機及車輛符合法定安全及服務標準，便應獲發牌照。透過市場自由競爭，不僅能確保車源充足，更能推動平台優化價格與服務。 二、 嚴格落實乘客安全保障機制 網約車合法化的核心在於保障市民安全。本人促請在附屬法例中加入或落實以下強制性安全措施： 1 嚴格准入審查： 規定網約車司機必須通過無刑事犯罪記錄（特別是性罪行及暴力罪行）及嚴重交通違例記錄的審查，確保司機素質。  2 商業乘客保險： 清晰訂明平台及車輛必須購買涵蓋「商業乘客運輸」的第三者責任保險，確保意外發生時乘客能獲得充分的法律與經濟保障。 3 利用科技提升安全： 強制應用程式內置「一鍵求助（SOS）」及即時行程分享功能；並在平衡私隱的前提下，鼓勵車廂安裝錄音及錄影設備，以便在發生糾紛時作為法律證據。 結語 網約車已是市民生活不可或缺的一部分。市民期望的是一個「安全、方便、具合理競爭」的交通網絡。促請委員會充分考慮廣大市民對增加網約車供應及加強安全保障的訴求。 不勝感激。 Escar Lai

name: 鄧卓豪
body: I am a Hong Kong resident and regular user of point-to-point transport. While Isupport the introduction of a clear regulatory framework for ride-hailing servicesunder the Road Traffic (Amendment) (Ride-hailing Service) Bill 2025 — whichbrings much-needed oversight on safety, insurance, driver standards, and platformaccountability — I strongly object to the proposed strict quota of only 10,000vehicle permits for all ride-hailing services combined, together with a lottery-basedallocation mechanism and an inadequately defined dynamic adjustment process. Hong Kong has a population of approximately 7.4 million in one of the world’sdensest urban environments. It already has around 18,163 licensed taxis. Despitethis fleet, passengers frequently encounter well-known shortcomings: trip refusals(especially for short journeys), limited availability during peak hours, evenings,weekends, adverse weather, or in the New Territories, and inconsistent servicequality. Properly regulated ride-hailing services address many of these gapsthrough convenient app-based booking, real-time GPS tracking, transparentpricing, passenger ratings, and often better reliability. The Transport and Logistics Bureau’s own estimate suggests that 10,000 vehicles(operating on average six hours per day with 12 trips each) could deliver roughly120,000 daily trips, close to the surveyed demand of around 114,000. Thiscalculation, however, appears overly conservative. It likely underestimates latentdemand previously suppressed by the absence of legal ride-hailing options, as wellas future growth from economic and tourism recovery, major events, shifting workpatterns, and the needs of residents in outlying areas. A cap set at roughly half thesize of the existing taxi fleet, for an innovative new sector that complements ratherthan replaces taxis, risks creating chronic undersupply from day one. The practical consequences for ordinary passengers will be significant: Longer wait times, particularly when demand spikes (rush hours, late nights, rainy days, after concerts or sports events, or for time-sensitive journeys such as medicalappointments or airport transfers). Upward pressure on fares due to reduced competition and persistent supplyshortages. Fewer meaningful choices in vehicle type, service level, or platformresponsiveness, stifling innovation (for example, wider deployment of electric orwheelchair-accessible vehicles). The proposed allocation mechanism compounds these concerns. A lottery systemis arbitrary and does not prioritise safety records, driving experience, vehiclestandards, or proven operational quality. It risks excluding capable drivers andoperators who have already invested in suitable vehicles or built service trackrecords, while creating unnecessary uncertainty. Any priority given to long-termprivate car owners should be secondary to clear, transparent, merit-based criteriafocused on passenger protection and service excellence. The dynamic adjustment mechanism is mentioned in principle but lacks sufficientdetail on review frequency, objective triggers, data sources, and transparency. Avague commitment to review “at a later stage” is insufficient. Hong Kong needs arobust, time-bound process — for example, quarterly reviews in the first 12–24months — using clear, publicly reported metrics such as average passenger waittimes (from platform data), vehicle utilisation rates, and complaint/surveyvolumes, with predefined thresholds that prompt quota increases. Without thesesafeguards, the system may remain slow to respond to real shortages. Hong Kong deserves a modern point-to-point transport ecosystem that enhanceschoice, reliability, and efficiency while maintaining proper safeguards. Anappropriately scaled ride-hailing sector would support flexible employment,improve the overall passenger experience, complement existing taxis and publictransport, and strengthen Hong Kong’s attractiveness as a place to live, work, andvisit.  I therefore urge the Legislative Council, when scrutinising the subsidiarylegislation, to: Substantially increase the initial vehicle permit quota to a level clearly capable ofmeeting demonstrated and near-term demand (industry proposals of 20,000+provide a more realistic starting point). Replace the lottery with a transparent, criteria-driven allocation system thatrewards safety, compliance, and service quality. Embed a strong, time-specific, and data-driven dynamic adjustment mechanismwith mandatory early reviews, public metrics, and clear escalation triggers. Regulation should protect the public interest without unnecessarily constrainingbeneficial competition and consumer choice. The current 10,000 cap, combinedwith lottery allocation and vague adjustment provisions, tilts too far towardrestriction and risks disappointing the very passengers the regime is meant toserve. Thank you for your consideration. I am happy to provide additional input or data ifrequired.

name: 吳志傑
body: 希望減少uber司機限制，讓更多人能夠成為uber司機，釋放需要彈性工作人事的勞動力，讓更多有時間限制的人加入勞動市場自力更生，例如放寬司機人數限制，放寬對車輛的要求，放寬對司機的要求。 政府不應偏坦的士司機，即使會導致的士牌照價格下降，這是因為當時批出的士牌照時成本是非常低的，其後的投機買賣，是市場的自由行為，政府不應保障這些投機炒風而犧牲社會的公眾利益。

name: TAM Inti
body: Dear Secretariat / Consultation Team, I am writing to formally submit my views in strong support of the legalisation andstrategic regulation of app-based ride-hailing services in Hong Kong. As a global financial hub and a leading smart city, Hong Kong's transportecosystem must evolve to meet the changing demands of its residents, businesscommunity, and international tourists. The integration of ride-hailing platforms is avital component of a modern, multi-modal transport network. To support the sustainable growth of this sector, I would like to offer the followingstrategic recommendations: Substantially Increase Licence Quotas: The current supply of point-to-pointtransport does not fully meet peak-demand periods, leading to prolonged waittimes and a diminished user experience. I strongly urge the Government to expandthe available quotas for ride-hailing vehicles. Increasing these numbers will fosterhealthy market competition, stabilise pricing, and ensure adequate coverage acrossall districts, including underserved suburban areas. Enhance Urban Mobility and Tourism Appeal: International business travellers andtourists heavily rely on digital, on-demand transport solutions. Providing aseamless, legally recognised ride-hailing framework is essential to maintainingHong Kong’s competitive edge as a world-class destination. Establish a Robust Regulatory and Safety Framework: Expanding the quota shouldgo hand-in-hand with strict safety standards. The Government should implement astreamlined registration system that requires comprehensive commercial insurance,mandatory vehicle safety checks, and background checks for all drivers.  Complement Existing Public Transport: Rather than replacing traditional taxis ormass transit, a well-regulated ride-hailing sector with expanded quotas fills acrucial gap in the market, particularly for point-to-point premium travel and off-peak commuting. By progressively increasing quotas and establishing a clear legal pathway for ride-hailing platforms, the Government can drive innovation, create flexible economicopportunities, and significantly elevate Hong Kong’s transport infrastructure. Thank you for considering these views. I look forward to seeing a progressivepolicy framework that supports Hong Kong's smart-city vision. Yours faithfully, Tam

name: HO Twinny
body: 不希望政府限制預約車輛的限額，因為平常返工的時段，在街道上根本截不到的士，我女兒每天也需要成Uber上學，如果天氣不穩定落大雨嘅日子，的士更是直接會要求附加費，加上新一代的士司機，大多用預約制度喜歡走長途路程，短途在街上是很難找到的士

name: 鄭恩浩
body: 現在全世界唯獨香港對網約車有這樣的規管。內地也可以有多個網約車平台供民眾選用。 擁抱大勢，放寛規管，讓市場自我調節。 車主只需要簡單登記便可以經營網約車。

name: TSANG Yuk
body: 一定是綱約好。自由到大 客人同司機都可有彈性。现今時代是各取所 需。的士 要交更  司機年齡 偏大。 發牌數量應該解剖三萬左右 先符合現金社會需求。 個個現代化城市已經普遍使用網約功能。

name: 方芷君
body: 本人支持Uber合法化，但如果該私人車輛是需要擁有其業權才可以進行網約服務，本人覺得非常不合理。 以的士為例，的士司機是不需要擁有該的士才可以進行服務，為什麼Uber司機反而需要？

name: 李健恩
body: 發牌太少至車費升高和選擇少了，的士司機年齡過高。

name: 梁嘉銓
body: 我支持網約車，香港是自由市場，市民應可有更多乘車選擇。當然要有適合的監管，和安全措施。

name: XU David
body: I am writing to urge the Legislative Council to formally recognize and supportUber as a vital and essential part of Hong Kong’s public transportation network. Ina modern, international city like Hong Kong, reliable, convenient, and user-friendly transport options are critical for residents, tourists, and the economy. Unlike the traditional taxi service, Uber provides a consistently professional andefficient alternative. Local taxis are frequently characterized by rude andunpleasant service, with many drivers operating old vehicles. A significant numberof taxi drivers have limited or no English proficiency, often requiring passengers toprovide directions, which creates unnecessary stress and inconvenience —particularly for visitors and non-Cantonese speakers. In contrast, Uber offers clean, modern vehicles, polite and professional drivers,reliable GPS navigation, transparent pricing, and easy booking through a user-friendly app. It fills important gaps in our public transport system, especiallyduring peak hours, in less accessible areas, or for those needing point-to-pointservice with confidence and predictability. I respectfully call on the Council to support regulatory measures that integrate andlegitimize ride-hailing services like Uber, ensuring they can operate fully and fairlyalongside traditional taxis. This will enhance overall mobility, boost tourism, andimprove the daily commuting experience for all Hong Kong residents. Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I look forward to yourpositive action in strengthening Hong Kong’s transport system. Yours sincerely

name: 鄧潔霞
body: 發牌10,000實在太少，車輛不足導致等候時間增長，出行車費也因供不應求而導致車費加價，對於需要乘車的市民來說實屬不便。請不要因為的士業界說”因為網約車而導致他們的生意減少”，而事實上是大部份的士的服務太差，而導致市民轉搭網約車，請不要本末倒置。發牌原是希望市民可光明正大的乘坐網約車，而不是左規管右規管車輛數目減少，導致市民更加選擇少、更加不便。所以請貴局重新考慮發牌的數目，以免影響市民。

name: AU YEUNG Wing-sze
body: 牌照quota太少起碼要2萬至2.5萬個才足夠 現在的建議 嚴重影響乘客的選擇而且價錢貴很多 的士司機年紀很多也很大 服務質素車齡也有待改善 政府不如也規管的士吧

name: 李欣芸
body: As a resident, I hope the government will issue more e-hailing licenses, similar tomainland China, where ride-hailing services are readily available anytime.Currently, it’s very difficult to hail a taxi, especially on rainy days. To makecommuting more convenient for the public, e-hailing services should be fullysupported through digital platforms. If only 10,000 licenses are issued, waiting times will become even longer, andfares will rise significantly. Hong Kong is an international city — yet our ride-hailing services risk falling behind Southeast Asian countries like Thailand andIndonesia, where booking a ride is already more affordable and efficient. Mostimportantly, we should not lag behind our motherland, the mainland of China,where e-hailing services are far more advanced and accessible. For Hong Kong to remain competitive and convenient for its citizens, 10,000licenses are clearly insufficient.

name: STRUGNELL Paul
body: Regarding the proposed cap of 10,000 licences for ride hailing service vehicles, Iurge you to consider increasing this cap. Sources have indicated that there are about 30,000 ride hailing vehicles currentlyin operation. Reducing this by two thirds will likely cause a severe shortage,leading to users like me being unable to hail a vehicle within a reasonable time andat a reasonable price. Surge pricing already causes huge price spikes at busy times. With the number oflicences limited to only 10,000 this existing problem will only be worsened.

name: 陳萬明
body: 10,000個名額根本太少。我個人認為政府不應該規管網約車，這是市場決定不是政府決定。對比其他國家包括中國大陸美國英國都沒有任何政府對網約車進行數量上的管控。有時汽車不單止是一個交通工具也可能是一家人的生存途徑。有時都可以是放工的兼職。請政府給底層小市民一條生路

name: AU Long-yin
body: I believe the proposed quota of 10,000 vehicle permits for ride-hailing services isinsufficient to meet public demand. Imposing such a strict cap could significantlylimit supply and negatively impact commuters’ daily travel needs. The government should carefully consider overall public interest rather thanprioritizing the protection of the traditional taxi industry. Policies that restrictcompetition risk leading to longer waiting times, higher fares, and fewer choicesfor passengers. Ride-hailing services play an important role in providing flexible, efficient, andaccessible transportation. Any regulatory framework should aim to balanceindustry development with consumer needs, ensuring a fair, competitiveenvironment that benefits the public at large.

name: WONG Hung-to
body: 請參考內地的做法，讓的士和網約車互相競爭。的士的便利性無可取代，沒必要限制網約車發牌數量。

name: 陳文德
body: -  10,000名額實在不足 -  建議30,000名額

name: 余璧君
body: 我贊成讓網約車uber合法化。 傳統的士的濫收車資、繞路、態度欠佳等問題非一日兩日的事，積癆成疾，現可明顯見到對旅遊業帶來負面影響，影響香港聲譽。因此提供多一個選項給市民及遊客會更好。

name: CHAN Hong-ki
body: 牌照只得10000個 絕對不能滿足現時乘客需求。 香港人甚或旅客外出絕大部分都會第一時間選擇網約車，是現代人的習慣了， 經網約平台可省去被的士司機濫收車資的常態，以建立香港智慧形都市形象，與時俱進。 建議政府考慮最少30000 牌照的網約車，因為其實網約車司機大部分都兼職工作，不是全職，要24小時全天候有充足網約車行駛，必須要30000個牌照以確保網約車費維持穩定，不會因牌照太少而供不應求，以致車費大幅上漲及增長乘客等候時間。  要令大眾可享受網約車利民的優點，請真的要改變政府守舊思想，採取積極措施！

name: LAM King
body: 如果限制在10000架網車，會形同好似的士車隊call車情況一樣，經常call唔到車，建議利用科技動態online數量維持10000架車，車隊call車统一在一個平台，同時不要抹殺兼職司機的貢獻，俾空間兼職司機的生存空間

name: 王文傑
body: 與其去限司機數量，更建議引入可加可減機制，監督平台收取佣金上限下限，以防止一到兩間平台以價格壟斷，之後再壓榨司機/轉架車費給乘客。 司機車資收入設下限，保障司機。 應付車資監管，保障乘客。

name: PANKAU Martin
body:
10,000 permission for ride hailing is insufficient for a city of 7.5 million people.There are only 18,163 licensed taxis available. More point to point transportationoptions are needed. Especially in bad weather. But also for choice. Ride hailingdrivers have nicer cars and just try harder than your average 60 year old cabbie.Unfriendly in a lot of cases too. Give them some competition, so that they tryharder to provide customers the service they need.  寄件者:
已傳送: 下午 7:05:07 (2026 年 6 月 9 日星期二) (UTC+08:00) Beijing, Chongqing, Hong Kong, Urumqi
收件者: sc_subleg <sc_subleg@legco.gov.hk>
主旨: Uber

let there be competition and that will bring down prices and improve service.

We are a city of service and we glow bright.

Don’t allow only one red group dominate.

Open the floodgates and let people choose.

Uber is amazing and now red taxis are improving too. It’s a great direction we are heading

LC Paper No. CB(3)662/2026(183)

name: 方海茵
body: 致：運輸及物流局 / 立法會交通事務委員會事由： 就網約車發牌上限嚴重不足、漠視市民選擇權及扼殺兩萬司機生計之意見書 本人為沒有私家車的普通市民，亦是網約車長期用戶。得知政府擬將網約車牌照首階段上限設為 10,000 個，本人深感憤慨與憂慮。此舉不僅剝奪市民出行選擇權，更生生扼殺了兩萬多名司機的生計。以下為本人之強烈訴求： 強行封頂扼殺兩萬司機生計，導致兩萬家庭陷入困境 目前全港有超過 30,000 名活躍網約車司機，強行將牌照限制在 10,000 個，意味着將有超過 20,000 名司機即時面臨失業。這些司機背後是兩萬個普通的香港家庭，他們依賴這份靈活收入供樓、養家、供書教學。 政府在沒有提供任何轉業過渡方案下，一刀切剝奪他們的生計，等同親手打碎兩萬個家庭的經濟支柱，將引發嚴重的基層失業與社會問題。 市民擁有選擇交通工具的權利，發牌不足導致求過於供香港市民與訪港旅客有權選擇高質素、點對點的出行服務。網約車透明的計價與優質態度，正補足了傳統交通的短板。配額嚴重不足，必然導致未來市民「叫車難」、候車時間倍增，變相懲罰市民。網約車與的士客路不同，非零和博弈數據與事實證明，網約車與的士的客路存在明顯分流。網約車用戶看重的是無現金支付、車廂整潔與不拒載的優質服務。 政府應以開放態度「擴大個人化交通市場」，而非透過強制縮減網約車數量來保護單一市場。 強烈訴求：本人促請政府正視兩萬個家庭的生計與百萬市民的出行權益，立即取消或大幅放寬 10,000 個牌照的非理性限制，改以市場供求作動態調整，切勿推出一項「雙輸」的規管政策。

name: KWAN Ka-man
body: 10000個 配額真係唔夠 仲要抽籤咁而家做緊嗰啲抽唔到咁點樣搵食呢？ 仲有要立法控制 平台收傭的比例

name: 鍾偉康
body: 10000個牌太少，限制住數量就無左網約車入行門檻低嘅原意。變咗第二個的士牌。

name: YIP Mimi
body: As a regular user of point-to-point transport services, I am concerned that anymeasures to limit the future supply of ride-hailing vehicles could negatively impactthe overall commuting experience for residents. A reduction in supply would likelylead to longer waiting times, higher fares, and fewer transportation options,especially during peak hours or in areas with limited taxi availability. Uber have introduced greater convenience, transparency, and service quality intothe market. They have provided meaningful competition, which in turn hasencouraged improvements across the broader transport sector. Restricting theirgrowth risks reversing these gains. Furthermore, limiting alternatives may inadvertently place passengers in a positionwhere they have fewer choices and must rely more heavily on traditional taxiservices. This could reduce service accountability and potentially worsenpassenger experience, particularly in terms of service attitude and reliability. I respectfully urge the Subcommittee to consider policies that promote faircompetition and consumer choice, rather than restricting supply. A balancedregulatory approach would help ensure that Hong Kong continues to offerefficient, high-quality, and user-friendly transport options for both residents andvisitors.

name: KWOK X
body: 1） 本人對政府建議僅發出一萬個網約車牌照表示極度不滿及強烈反對。 香港市民每日出行需求龐大，而的士服務長期質素低劣，服務態度差劣，早已令市民失望透頂。相反，網約車平台Uber 提供快捷、可靠、服務優質嘅選擇，深受市民信任。本人亦經常使用Uber，因為其效率同服務遠遠優於的士。 然而，政府僅計劃發出一萬個牌照，根本不足以應付市場龐大需求。此舉不單止無法改善交通問題，更會嚴重限制市民選擇，直接影響日常生活便利。市民需要嘅係更多元化、可靠嘅出行方式，而唔係被迫依賴一個多年來未能改善嘅的士制度。 本人在此嚴正要求立法會必須正視市民聲音，立即重新檢視有關政策，大幅增加網約車牌照 數量，確保交通服務真正符合香港市民嘅期望。若政府繼續漠視民意，必將進一步削弱市民對公共政策嘅信任或社會更大不滿。 市民需要選擇，市民需要尊重。請立法會切勿再以低效政策犧牲市民福祉。

name: 容先生
body: 我認為網約車的牌照發放數量應該要比預期中多一點，政府可能低估了一般市民大眾對網約車的需求，若果限制了網絡牌照的發放，有些平台可能會趁機抬高網約車的價錢，最後受苦的也是市民。此外，許多網約車司機也是以駕駛網約車維持生計維持生計，因此他們的牌照費用不宜定得太高，以免為他們的生活構成負擔

name: 陳永健
body: 現時的網約車不應優先獲得牌照，因他們現在是在做非法的事情。牌照應以公開抽籤的方式分配。

name: 陸俊軒
body: 關於優化網約車合法化及放寬牌照配額的政策建議 十分認同政府將網約車納入合法規管的方針，這絕對是推動智慧出行、提升市民生活質素的重要一步。然而，要真正發揮網約車的社會效益，促請政府務必審慎評估並加大網約車車輛許可證的配額上限。 若將牌照硬性限制在較低水平（例如 10,000 個），恐會衍生以下連鎖效應，與政策初衷背道而馳：  供求失衡與價格飆升： 配額過少將直接導致市場供不應求。在尖峰時段，市民不僅面臨候車時間過長的問題，更需承擔因供需失調而引致的高昂車資，加重市民日常出行負擔。  加劇路面交通壓力： 當網約車因「車少價高」而失去便利性與經濟效益時，部分有出行需要的市民將被迫選擇自行駕駛私家車外。屆時路面私家車數量不減反增，勢必加劇本港原本已十分嚴峻的交通擠塞問題。 總結建議： 一個健康且具韌性的公共交通網絡，需要充足的運力支撐。建議政府實施彈性配額機制，根據市場實際需求、市民候車大數據及交通承載力，逐步且適度地放寬許可證上限。此舉既能引入良性競爭、穩定服務價格，亦能鼓勵市民實踐「減少養車、多搭公共交通」的環保出行模式，從根本上優化本港的路面交通網絡。

name: LAI Keith
body: 敬啟者：立法會 本人對政府僅發出一萬個網約車牌照表示嚴正反對。此數量本身已經嚴重不足，更加要分配俾多個平台，意味著每個平台實際可用牌照更少，市民選擇被進一步削弱。 的士服務多年來質素低劣，市民早已失望。相反，Uber 等網約車平台提供快捷、可靠、服務優質嘅出行方式，深受市民依賴。若只限一萬個牌照，仲要瓜分，根本無法滿足龐大需求，嚴重影響市民日常生活便利。 本人要求立法會必須立即修訂政策，大幅增加網約車牌照數量，並確保分配公平合理，真正尊重市民需求。 此致敬禮

name: 石玉暉
body: 網約車服務非常滿意及方便，而且多個國家都有這種網約車服務，而且香港亦都得國際大都會，這個服務真的是適合全世界

name: 萬青
body: 本人在香港生活工作十九年，认为香港最落后于世界主要大都市的就是出租车服务，具体表现在：车型旧、车脏、司机态度很差、开车非常快不考虑乘客的感受，很多车至今仍无法电子支付。对比亚洲其它大城市，车费也很昂贵。 这几年Uber的推出，让香港市民出行有了更多的选择，更好的服务。政府应该更多地鼓励Uber和网约车，而非设置限制，特别是数量的限制。 作为市场经济自由的城市，只要乘客受益，司机愿意，就应该大力推广，而非添加种种限制。任何的限制都是行业小圈子的自我保护，对广大市民无益，影响香港这个亚洲金融中心的形象。

name: 劉志光
body: 規限網約車司機數目變相幫助的士。叫唔到車。網約車條例已經比大陸及其他國家落後10多年。而家立法想清楚啲啦。而家呢位局長真係唔係好掂。

name: 梁建棟
body: 希望無限制數量發牌，方便市民及遊客出行。

name: 伍志偉
body: 我覺得不應該只發1萬個牌，因為這樣只會弄巧反拙，令到服務變差，應該向現在有的司機發牌，先有意思；同埋不應該要司機另外買份保險，因為的士都係車行負責買。仲有的士又改優勢，佢扣分自己扣的士牌，即使佢扣晒的士牌所有分佢可以改轉做uber, 所以應該考慮網約車同的士扣分制都一樣先會公平一啲，因為而家甚麼都同的士做比較。其實增加網約車發牌都不會影響的士，原因係Uber根本現在都生存緊同埋Uber和的士的客群不同，搭Uber嗰啲係根本唔會搭的士

name: CHEN Cecilia-surong
body: I support ride hailing services to be legalised and properly regulated in HongKong.

name: BAJAJ Jason
body: Do not restrict licenses. Make sure there are as many UBER and Didi drivers inHK and let them compete for customer service. HK Taxi drivers of qualifiedshould also become drivers or give better service. Eliminating and restricting ridesharing drivers is not the solution. Young people can become drivers, get decentincome - the old HK taxi drivers should not hurt the economy or job opportunitiesof others.

name: YAU Libra
body: 作為香港市民，我對現時擬議條款中多項過於嚴苛、缺乏彈性的限制(特別是設定 10,000 個過低的車輛牌照上限)持強烈反對及保留態度。現特此提交意見書，促請政府重審方案，以保障市民出行選擇權。 現時香港市民及訪港旅客對網約車服務需求極其殷切。擬議將許可證數目限制在 10,000 個，遠遠低於市場實際運作中的活躍車輛數目。即時是現時的標準，在繁忙時，特別是下雨天時已經很難叫車。在設定車輛牌照上限(10,000 個) 後，將直接導致乘客等候時間大幅延長、繁忙時段車費飆升，尤其會對公共交通不足的偏遠地區（我居住的地方就是）居民造成嚴重不便。 我強烈建議特區政府重新審視並調高初始網約車許可證發牌上限，並以不低於已有活躍車輛數目為下限。 感謝 閣下撥冗參閱此意見書，期望立法會及政府能採納市民真正聲音，制定更平衡、包容的方案。

name: 岑子傑
body: 本人希望立法網約車，就跟中國大陸一樣網約車合法化，希望可以給市民多一個選擇

name: 許甘
body: 本人強烈反對將網約車配額上限僅設為 10,000 個。 此政策顯然是向多年來壟斷市場、把持既得利益的的士車主與業界傾斜。的士牌照多年來被形同民生炒賣工具，部分既得利益者為了維護自身利益，長期抗拒市場競爭，導致香港市民忍受了多年「拒載、揀客、搭車難」的痛苦。 網約車的出現，本是打破壟斷、體現共享經濟的良藥。若政府為安撫的士業界，硬性將配額壓至 1 萬張，現有運力將暴跌逾六成，車費更恐暴升七成。這等同用公眾福祉去補貼壟斷階層，令香港交通退步。 政府必須打破保護主義，以市民利益為先，將配額提高至更具彈性的 30000張，還市民真正進步的出行選擇。

name: 秦玉新
body: Uber平台自我規管已經非常棒，致府大可不必為咗臭名遠揚嘅香港的士行業而做咁多無謂野，支持Uber

name: YEUNG Kitty
body: I think regulating ride-hailing and limiting to 10,000 does not make sense at all. I like Uber service and it works very well. HK is a free economy, and whyLegislative Council has so much time to waste on regulating ride-hailing servicesfrom different platforms? Given recently so many traffic accidents in normal taxis and high age of taxidrivers, this should be the focus for Legislative Council.

name: 梁桓明
body: 本人認為新［綱約車］數量應該設定在20,000~25,000架之間，才滿足現在本地居民及到港旅客的最低需求數量。

name: YEUNG Carrie
body: 全世界都咁普及嘅網絡車，唔知點解你哋政府要搞咁多規矩咁多要求，現在沒太多冇規矩嘅時候人，哋而家都用緊啦！行駛中簡直阻人哋搵食，浪費人力物力拖延咁耐，浪費納稅人既金錢，陳美寶絕絕是失敗，簡單事情覆習化，縱容的士司機逃稅，呃市民車資，我相信只有的士公司及的士司機會反對，市民基本也一直都支持網約車，呢個係市民自由選擇，唔好再訂多問題去阻人搵食，香港已經好難搵食，連唯一生存都阻撓，又要考試，基本上有車人士全部也要考了報史及筆試，政府已經又夾硬要抽水考試費，真係太無腦太差

name: SO Manson
body: Imposing a rigid 10,000-permit cap on Hong Kong’s new ride-hailing frameworkis a step backwards for a city striving to be a smart global hub, and it will directlydisrupt our day-to-day commutes. It is already an agonising, daily struggle to hail atraditional cab during rush hours, rainy days, or shift changes. Artificially chokingthe supply of alternative point-to-point transit will not magically improve the taxiindustry; it will simply result in longer wait times, skyrocketing surge pricing, andthousands of stranded commuters left on the curbside. A healthy, competitive market requires dynamic, consumer-driven supply, not aprotectionist ceiling. We only need to look at our neighbours to see how otherjurisdictions have navigated this transition. China’s major cities successfullyintegrated massive ride-hailing networks alongside traditional taxis by lettingmarket demand dictate supply, dramatically improving urban mobility. Singaporeadopted a progressive, unified regulatory structure for both street-hail and ride-hailoperators, focusing on safety and service standards rather than arbitrary vehiclequotas, which keeps their transport ecosystem highly responsive and efficient.Conversely, South Korea’s rigid, restrictive approach and outright bans on non-taxiplatforms caused massive late-night transit shortages in Seoul, forcing thegovernment to constantly scramble with reactive interventions.  By pushing forward with a strict 10,000 quota, Hong Kong risks repeating SouthKorea's friction rather than replicating Singapore's balance or China's efficiency.The government's assumption that 10,000 vehicles can sufficiently cover 120,000daily trips ignores the reality of peak-hour bottlenecks. If Hong Kong truly wants amodern, world-class transport system, it must scrap this arbitrary cap and letgenuine public demand shape the market.

name: 劉凱祺
body: 起碼要3-4萬個牌先合理，要供過於求品質先會提升

name: 徐躍峰
body: 本人認爲1萬個網約車牌照數量太少，嚴重影響市民的出行，市道本來就艱難，這樣少的牌照數量，一定會造成車費大幅上升，本人每天需要使用網約車服務3-4次，强烈建議政府增加牌照數量。

name: 李伟雄
body: 发牌限制是违背共亨的原則！

name: MASSEY Andrew
body: Statement in Support of Uncapped Ride-Sharing Services in Hong Kong We are living in the 21st century. Hong Kong has long prided itself on being Asia'sworld city, a place that champions progressive outlooks, open competition, andforward-thinking governance. That progressiveness is precisely what gave HongKong its strength and success: free markets, consumer choice, and the relentlesspursuit of excellence over protectionism. Yet when it comes to urban transport, Hong Kong is embarrassingly stuck in thepast. The Hong Kong Taxi Owners' Association Limited and its counterparts havemismanaged their civil duty to such an extent that they no longer deserveexclusivity in this 21st century free market. Their services are appalling. Elderlydrivers who are clearly not fit to drive continue to operate, as evidenced by thealarming number of crashes and accidents, some of them fatal. Bad and aggressivedriving is routine. Vehicles are old, poorly maintained, and not kept up to date.Service is rude, with drivers selectively choosing whom to pick up, overchargingpassengers, and declining to drive to destinations they are duty bound to serve. These associations are corrupt, hiding behind protectionism within the Hong Konggovernment, which is shocking and shameful. It reflects extremely badly on theHong Kong government establishment. This obvious grift is at risk of underminingpublic trust in the government itself, because citizens can see exactly what ishappening: a privileged cartel being shielded from competition while the publicsuffers. What is seemingly being overlooked in this entire debate is the end customer: thepeople of Hong Kong, whom taxi and ride-sharing services exist to serve. Whatgood does limiting vehicle numbers do except to frustrate ordinary people anddrive up prices through artificial scarcity and overcharging? It also pushes moreresidents to buy private cars and hire drivers who then illegally double park and clog up the roads, making traffic worse for everyone. This needs to be penalised,whilst the government should be promoting multi-person vehicular use thatactually reduces congestion. Safety should be paramount. There must be a mandatory retirement age of 65 yearsold for all commercial drivers. There must be regular inspections of all commercialvehicles, including the installation of tachometers to ensure drivers have not beenspeeding, driving dangerously, or operating without adequate rest and indication. Anything less than this will cause a public outcry that the government is not actingin the best interests of the people, and is implicit in some kind of corruption. Thetime for reform is now. Open the market to ride-sharing services, hold theincumbent taxi industry to proper standards, and put the citizens of Hong Kongfirst.

name: 施詠
body: 本人認為要把Uber發牌數目增加 增加業內競爭 提高司機及服務的質素 若果過分限制Uber的發牌數量 長遠而言，只有對現金冇禮貌的的士司機有好處 對市民而言 除了服務態度 以及方便程度 也是百害而無一利 而Uber的Apps 還可以對司機作出評分及意見 就算遺留了東西在車上面 平台也能協助市民提供服務 與傳統的的士服務比較下 具有非常的優勢

name: 林卓聞
body: Dear Chairman and Members of the Subcommittee, I am writing to formally submit my views regarding the four pieces of subsidiarylegislation currently under review by the Subcommittee concerning the regulationof ride-hailing services in the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. While I support the Government’s initiatives to establish a clear legal frameworkthat ensures passenger safety, vehicle insurance coverage, and high quality ofservice, I would like to express a critical recommendation regarding the supply andmanagement of ride-hailing vehicle licenses or quotas. To preserve Hong Kong's current urban mobility standards and ensure publicconvenience, it is vital that the Government increases the total allowable numberof authorized ride-hailing services/vehicles, rather than imposing restrictive capsthat fail to meet actual market demand. My recommendation is based on the following key considerations: Preserving Current Service Levels and Public Convenience: Ride-hailing services have become an integral part of Hong Kong's daily transportecosystem, filling critical gaps in point-to-point transit, especially during peakhours, inclement weather, and in areas underserved by traditional public transport.Restricting the number of legal ride-hailing vehicles below existing marketcapacity will inevitably lead to longer passenger wait times, a drop in servicereliability, and severe public inconvenience. Mitigating "Surge Pricing" and Inflated Consumer Costs: Basic economic principles dictate that if the supply of authorized vehicles is kept artificially low while demand remains high, consumers will face significantlyhigher fare prices through dynamic surge pricing. Maintaining a higher totalnumber of available services ensures healthy market competition and keeps point-to-point transit affordable for everyday citizens. Supporting Hong Kong’s Tourism and "Smart City" Ambitions: As an international financial hub and a premier tourist destination, Hong Kongrelies heavily on seamless, digital-first transport solutions. Modern travelers expectrobust, readily available ride-hailing options. A restrictive quota would hinder ouralignment with global "Smart City" benchmarks and negatively impact the visitorexperience. Conclusion & Recommendation To successfully integrate ride-hailing services into Hong Kong's regulatedtransport framework without disrupting the city's efficiency, I strongly urge theSubcommittee to recommend a flexible, demand-led licensing mechanism. Thetotal number of permitted ride-hailing vehicles must be scaled upward from currentbaseline usage to effectively maintain—and future-proof—our city's excellentservice standards. Thank you for your time, consideration, and dedication to refining this vital pieceof legislation. I am comfortable with this submission being uploaded to theLegislative Council website for public viewing. Yours faithfully, Sherman

name: RIDGERS William
body: The Hong Kong government has a duty to serve its citizens, businesses andvisitors. Ride hailing services have improved the transport infrastructure of HongKong immeasurably, allowing people much better choice, much lower waitingtimes and access to destinations that taxis often disdain. Ride-hailing vehicles areon average more modern, cleaner and more spacious than taxis. Rickety old redand green taxis, we should admit, are an embarrassment for those who have visitedany modern, international city.  If Hong Kong aspires to be a modern, technologically advanced city, it would notartificially curtail the number of ride hailing vehicles available. It would allowconsumers to make their own choice of which service they prefer.

name: CHAN Kwok-chu
body: 支持網約車合法化，能為司機提供**合法保障與穩定收入**。透過完善的法規監管與平台派單機制，司機不只能免於罰款風險，還能獲得源源不絕的客源。這不僅能優化大眾出行體驗，更為無數家庭帶來安心、可持續的經濟支柱。 支持維持現有3萬牌照 足夠市民所需。

name: LEE Joseph
body: Honourable Members, I urge you to reconsider the proposal to cap ridesharing atonly 10,000 licences, as this restriction directly harms the public you serve. HongKong residents have long endured inconsistent taxi service, poorly maintainedvehicles, and drivers whose behaviour too often falls short of acceptable standards.Ridesharing has filled this gap by offering cleaner cars, better service quality, anda level of reliability that many citizens now depend on. Around the world,ridesharing is recognised as the future of urban mobility, yet Hong Kong risksfalling behind by protecting outdated interests. If this policy is being shaped tosafeguard the financial positions of a small group of taxipermit holders rather thanthe needs of millions of ordinary residents, it would be a grave mistake - one thatthe public will remember for a very long time. Hong Kong deserves a modern,competitive, and customerfocused transport system, not artificial limits that holdthe city back.

name: 李敏儀
body: 支持Urban，因為價錢透明，車輛環境清潔，司機態度大多良好。

name: LOK Louisa
body: 網約車服務係每一個國際城市必有嘅service，而且比的士方便。 香港的士啲質素越嚟越差，司機揸車又chok，架車又臭，啲司機又冇禮貌成日俾面色人睇，我見唔到佢會有改善嘅一日。就算Uber貴啲我都會選擇搭Uber。 香港係一個國際城市成日都會有好多外國遊客嚟香港，有Uber對遊客係會方便好多，唔使怕俾人呃，order架車嘅時候已經心中有數係幾多錢，唔怕語言溝通問題，就算我自己喺外國都係成日用網約車Service安心好多。

name: RANGANI Pitamber
body: I personal and my family use Uber regularly and my parents are elderly. It is sodisappointing HK is keeping the cap of only 10,000 Ubers and yet HK calls itself a“ free market “. This is totally the opposite of a free market and my parents whoare seniors use uber often as they are more reliable and kinder.
